posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked: In the past week I have seen posts on IG of '15 Cristal and '14 Cristal Rose. One of those posts was from Essi Avellan who is a Champagne reviewer and was tasting both of those at the House with JBL. There has been a lot of discussion here about '14 Cristal not being the best release. I believe the grapes for the rose come from different plots so maybe it will be different quality. I have heard concerns with the '15 vintage generally being too ripe and developing some odd, vegetal flavors in bottle. Will be interesting to see how '15 Cristal has evolved. I guess the only thing we know for sure is that neither will be cheap.

posts.name: Dinesh Goyal
posts.cooked:
Here is an offline view from one of the top trade champagne buyers

“ Going forwards, I would tread carefully with champagne purchasing. While there will always be top wines produced in the region, the region is not without its challenges. After 2013, we do not have a vintage that we can call iconic until we get to 2018. This is less of an issue for the grower champagnes, who are making individual wines that highlight terroir or a particular grape more than a blended, house style. These wines tend to be made in much smaller volumes than the big houses and demand will still outstrip supply for the top growers, even in a recession. In many ways they act more like the top wines from burgundy.

The same cannot be said for the big houses, who have big contracts to honour and will likely need to put out vintage releases 2014-2017, to generate cash flow. These big houses do a good job of concealing how much wine they actually produce, and my concern is that while demand for champagne is high now, that will drop off quite quickly in the next year or two and prices will likely fall back around 20-25% in the top prestige cuvees”

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
Thank you for posting this, very interesting.

At the moment it seems to me champagne producers, both growers and maisons are doing everything possible to alienate loyal and long standing customers with incredulous price increases.

We were selling the RD 2007 Bollinger at 210 € and with the 2008 we are up to 350 to 400 €. Profiled, individual articles by William Kelley on the wine advocate and Antonio Galloni on Vinous might create an initial buzz but this has little long term meaning, Bollinger is not Krug or Cristal or even Dom Perignon.

With grower champagne, I feel we are losing the ground under our feet. Burgundy comparisons are apt in the sense of how some champagnes are made, in Burgundy we have good track records of how wines age. In the Champagne we just do not know how these champagnes with no tradition are going to hold up to the test of time. Add to this the whole no/low dosage dilemna and reduced sulphur, I feel those who are paying OTT prices are going to get a rude awakening.

Two customers independently compliaining about Ulysse Collin, (I did not sell them the bottles). Prices are in no way synchronised with what is going on in the bottle, the higher the price the higher the expectations and the champagnes delivered in the region of 60-90 € but not at 200 € plus.

Just the other day, I had this big, strong, beautiful man, come up to me, tears in his face, and say Sir, Donald, this Ulysse Collin - can you believe it, they’re crying - this Ulysse Collin, these champagnes that are so expensive and, this was a big guy, wouldn’t want to fight him, and I just said, you know, we’ve gotta make champagne great again, and they were just crying and these are tough champagne consumers, strong champagne consumers and you know, this Ulysse Collin, and I just hate talking about him, but these tough beautiful people keep coming up to me crying and you know, they’ve fought hard, worked years to buy grower champagnes and they’re crying, because the Collin wines are a little overpriced and it’s a disgrace and they’re crying, and I tell you, gotta make grower champagne great again.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
You know Greg, I really love you, but the thing is, I just don’t give a shit about Ulysse Collin. The thing that really bothers me is because I sell grower champagne everybody thinks one must be enamoured with this guy and his soulless swill and then all the sob stories and the time I waste explaining the whole saga. So these forums become a vent for my demented soul

Don’t get me wrongI am a cynical bugger, I spent my youth reading Villion, Heine and Catull and I’ve never gotten it out of my system, I have no problem charging usurious prices for something I consider worthless, when ever I get UC. .
William Kelley is a great guy and I’ve found my peace with him, he helps me sell the stuff I don’t understand. And then boom, he post this, not 100% factually true, but never the less great stuff, has the emotioanl veneer Galloni likes to use when it gets hard , but really solid writing. https://www.instagram.com/p/CpUduf5t2IA/

and the price goes up even more. I say to the customers, write to WK, he will respond. He’s got your back when it comes to prices, he can explain it in far more mellifluous jargon than I can.

Take consolation, then there is Greg on Wine Beserkers, He says, its market forces at work, your paying for something that is not even worth 70 bucks but the markets says otherwise,. And then they say, it costed 300 bucks and it did not taste like a 300 buck champagnes, I could have got a Krug for that.

I see the tears in their eyes, why are you crying, I ask? Do you not believe in markets, in the critics, the points. Don’t dare trust your own taste, thats a big mistake, you’ll be under paying for unvalidated champagne that might actually give you enjoyment.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
There has to be balance in the universe otherwise it gets too one sided. I respect your taste and opinions but you have to understand, for everyone who likes a particular champagne, there is someone else who does not.
And lastly someone has to tell the emperor, he has no clothes on.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
On a more serious note. I worked a long time in the michelin gastronomy. You have a wide variety of customers, but from my perspective the most special customers , were those that saved up for years, just to expereince michelin gastronomy once, a wedding anniversary, birthday or just to have dined once in a michelin restaurant. Such customers are very easy to spot and in every restaurant we worked in, we went out of our way to make it a really special occasion.

I see this in champagne, some one buying a case of cristal or bollinger or a DP for 1000 € + does not care about the money. But you have wine drinkers, who once in a lifetime want to taste a Krug or a Cristal, and I think here one has to be especially careful. I think this is something testers totally underestimate and do not really associate with. Champagne is my business, I believe I taste more champagnes than W. Kelley and Galloni in a year, and I have the overview. I don’t have it for Burgundy or other regions . And for the above reason, I think these weak and overpriced champagnes must called out for what they are.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I hope you won’t consider this a breach of the peace, Donald, and I have hitherto refrained from getting into this discussion on the grounds that, as the French say, les goûts et les couleurs ne se discutent pas, and that to each their own. But why don’t you like Ulysse Collin? And I’m talking about the wines, not the distribution system. I’m all in favor of robust exchange, and every proposition should be interrogated, but you’ve given us a lot of invective without much in the way of analysis. I could understand not liking the style, but “worthless swill”?

For my part, I love the wines for their texture and intensity of flavor; their aromas, patinated by élevage and reserve wines but without the exaggerations of oxidation or dominant oak; for their tension and precision without any excess of asperity; and for the relentless work that has gone into making them better every year for the more than a decade that I’ve been visiting and tasting. As someone who has sometimes criticized growers for disgorging too soon and shipping too soon after disgorgement, surely you can at least appreciate Olivier’s initiatives in that regard? Or his decision to constitute such large stocks of reserve wines? To me, it doesn’t really make sense to compare this unfavorably to a big house that only owns just over 30 hectares of vineyards, a fraction of its requirements; that outsources its viticulture to Veuve Cliquot; and that less than a decade ago were still using herbicides in the vineyard that produced at the time the most expensive wine in Champagne. As Michel Bettane once wrote of Egly, it would be unreasonable to expect big houses to be able to attain the same level of agronomic and technical excellence given their larger scale, and I don’t make these points even to criticize, just to say that we are comparing chalk and cheese.

Indeed, the most intelligent big houses today have understood that to compete they must become great farmers and pivot to a different approach: Roederer and Bollinger have been on this path for some time; and now DP, for example, are phasing out the luminous bottles and moving towards more and more late disgorgement, clearly reorienting their brand. As more and more small growers decide to keep their best grapes for themselves, and as a more extreme climate throws into relief differences in farming approaches, big houses dependent on purchased fruit and must are going to have a harder and harder time applying the requisite pixie dust in the cellar to maintain quality, and it is going to be very interesting to see where we end up in a decade.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I think this is quite likely right and some of the aggressive “repositioning” in pricing of many prestige cuvées right now is not very well timed. One was already paying a big premium for brand + bubbles, and it is difficult to digest big hikes. As for whether 2018 will be an “iconic” vintage, well, we will see about that…

There has to be balance in the universe otherwise it gets too one sided. I respect your taste and opinions but you have to understand, for everyone who likes a particular champagne, there is someone else who does not.

Donald, I can only wish you bona fortuna on your deeply quixotic (and clearly, for whatever reason, intensely personal) tilt at the Ulysse Collin windmill.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked:
That will run closer to $285 if brought back to the States, but still expensive. Magnums are basically 2x the 750’s. If I were going to buy more, it would be the magnums. I got an offer back in September for $210 per bottle, but it was only from 1 broker. I agree with Michael that this is the new normal price for high-end Champagne.

I did see Charles in November at La Fête/Philipponnat dinner in NYC and he told me that he thought the '12 vintage was the best in Champagne in recent memory. I took that to mean he thought his '12 was better than his '13, but I may be reading too much into that. In any case, I don’t buy a lot of Goisses, because it is one of the Champagnes that never seems to be ready. Having said that the '12 we had at the dinner was drinking well, maybe the other new normal for Champagne, drinking better younger. I do buy the 1522, which I think is a great value.

posts.name: Daniel Bailey
posts.cooked: Never ready or completely shot a few years after release as loads of the 96s were.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked: Not in our lifetimes, but might be worth it from a spec buy perspective. Rare to see magnums at 2x anymore.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked:
The '96 Goisses original release (both disgorgements) was overrated IMO. We did drink the '96 L.V. from magnum at the dinner and it far exceeded the original. Again, paraphrasing Charles, he basically said the ‘96 vintage was fragile. Yes there was a lot of acidity and a lot of ripeness (although that depended on when you picked), some wines had a tendency to oxidize than "normal’. The sooner they saw any oxygen (disgorgment) the sooner that clock started ticking. The L.V. was disgorged in 2020 I believe and it was a completely different Champagne, very enjoyable.

I have noticed this with plenty of other '96’s, lot’s of bottle variation in my experience. Some bottles can be amazing, others drink like pre-moxed white Burgundy.

posts.name: Daniel Bailey
posts.cooked: With the 12 comtes now back below £800/6, I’d take that every time over the goisses 13.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked: I bought more last week and yesterday. It reminds me of '06 Comtes, it was so cheap but it was available in quantity for a very long time. It was a screaming buy to me and other folks here. Maybe not the best Comtes ever made, but it surely has drunk well over the years. While '12 is much more expensive than '06 was, it is all relative and I think it is a better Champagne.

posts.name: Dinesh Goyal
posts.cooked: Jon - what’s your rank order of post 2000 cdc vintages?

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
I went back for a bit more 12 also.

I’d go:

08
12
02
04
06
07
11

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked:
I would go '08/‘12’/'02/'06/'04/'07. The first 3 are very close, '06 not far behind and the last 2 are basically tied with each other, but a bit behind the rest. I know some people love the last 2, and I like them, but they are not as rich as the others which I why I love Comtes. I bought all of these vintages on release (I own more Comtes than anything else) and Comtes drinks better on release than any other Champagne IMO.

For current consumption, I would drink '02/'06 and buy enough '12 so you can drink some now and follow it. I think there is more upside with the others and would sit on those unless you have enough to drink a bottle now and then.

Btw, if you take out the lowest price on WSP, '06 and '08 are basically trading for the same price in the US and '12 is the cheapest. '06 is a lot cheaper in the UK.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: Ive drank by far the most 06 of the recent vintages and it’s always struck me as fine but nothing all that special. I know a lot of people like it. I opened a bottle at the di fara event and people almost universally liked the 02 couche sensation more, although that’s a fantastic champagne.
